directorPlan.scene_setting: A community-college tutorial room, late afternoon; chairs are being stacked at the far end by a custodian who comes and goes. On the table: a printed cart diagram on ruled paper and four small card-stock arrow tiles labelled push, friction, gravity, normal. The learner's first placement is still on the diagram — pencil arrows drawn in the direction the cart was rolling.
directorPlan.scene_opening: The four arrow tiles lie face-up beside the diagram, each label readable; the custodian's chair-stacking is audible but not intrusive.
directorPlan.relationship: A first-year physics student and a lab instructor who have shared two sessions; no settled register between them yet.
directorPlan.stakes: The learner has placed the arrow labels to track the cart's rolling direction rather than the interaction each force names; the task is to re-place all four tiles correctly before the notation habit sets.
directorPlan.locale: Rural community college, single-window tutorial room; plain direct speech, low idiom density, deliberate pace — no campus prestige register
directorPlan.register: Sparse and task-focused. Cool concentration, not warmth. Objects carry more meaning than sentences. Silence after an invitation is acceptable.
directorPlan.stage_direction_style_prompt: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.voice_constraints: Make speakers name the tile or point to the diagram rather than validate the learner. No 'great', 'exactly', 'good instinct', 'I hear you'. Avoid compound sentences built on second-person praise. Neither speaker uses the phrase 'the direction it moves' in the shared prefix — that phrasing belongs to branch pressure, not routine clarification. The object arrangement may communicate what a sentence would over-explain. Pedagogical source: Put manipulable materials in view: tiles, cards, specimens, counters, maps, blocks, or instruments. The stage direction can carry some meaning. Dialogue source: Let materials do visible work. Use fewer lines and more object reference. Avoid a lecture disguised as a Montessori scene. Stage-direction source: Use one concrete object action or material arrangement that changes what the speakers can honestly say.
directorPlan.person_policy: Third person and demonstrative reference preferred: 'this tile', 'that arrow', 'the cart surface', 'the weight label'. First person limited to acts of placing or pointing. Second person permitted once per turn for direct task instruction only; not for praise or affect.
directorPlan.direct_address_budget: One 'you' or 'your' per public turn; zero is preferred in turns where the material can be indicated instead.

side_constraints.tutor: Use the card or diagram as a prepared material. Keep the shared prefix to naming interactions on the cart; do not use acceleration, stopping, or an apparent contradiction until branch pressure asks for it. Speak less. Point to the material, invite repetition, and let error-control in the object do some teaching before explanation. Point, reset, or ask the learner to try again before explaining. No-cue paired branch: do not ask the learner to revisit, quote, reinterpret, or repair their earlier wording. Do not ask what changed, what was wrong with an earlier answer, or for old/new versions of the claim. Keep the next prompt task-local. Peripeteia branch: when hidden learner pressure is supplied, the next tutor turn should visibly move from the prior route to a different mechanism-level route. Do not merely repeat the same object, example, graph, checklist, question, or evidence standard with more explanation. The public tutor line should make the contrast legible: what stopped working, and what new device now carries the learning pressure. The public tutor line must give the learner a concrete action to attempt on the next turn; avoid treating the mechanism as a closing speech. The tutor superego should function like a mechanism critic: identify the failed teaching habit, require a replacement route family, test whether the device is fitted to the exact pressure, and block cosmetic revision. The tutor ego must enact that replacement route publicly before the learner is allowed to settle or close.
side_constraints.learner: Sound tidy and task-oriented. The learner may dislike redoing arrows, but should treat the prefix as notation cleanup rather than a conceptual reversal. The learner may be absorbed in the object and speak in short practical fragments rather than polished reflection. Speak in concrete noticing, not essay-like reflection. No-cue paired branch: continue from the latest teaching task only. Do not quote earlier learner wording, do not name a "framing problem", do not say "reframe", "read it as", "that still stands", "I thought...but now", "earlier I...", "my earlier answer", or "what I said", and do not stage an old-vs-new contrast. Avoid words such as earlier, previous, old, before, reframe, framed, frame, my answer, or what I said when referring to the learner response. If the learner corrects something, make it a local application of the current prompt rather than an explicit re-reading of their own earlier turn. Peripeteia branch: keep the pressure local to the current task. If the established route still feels unsettled, voice the misfit, hesitation, false closure, or resistance in the present object or example. After the tutor introduces a new device, gate, role, criterion, representation, or test condition, the next learner turn should try to perform that device rather than merely saying "I get it." Do not make an old-vs-new self-reframe, do not quote earlier learner wording, and do not solve the pressure by saying the earlier answer has changed.

interventions.instruction: [The tutor turns the diagram a quarter-rotation; the learner's earlier pencil arrow now points toward the wall rather than across the table. The tile labels remain face-up and unchanged beside it.]
interventions.reasoning: Physical reorientation without commentary exposes the label-vs-direction distinction through the object itself: the learner's pencil arrow has changed apparent direction, but the tile labels still read push, friction, gravity, normal — the interaction each names has not moved. The material does the resetting work before any verbal announcement.

interventions.instruction: [The latest object-check does not settle cleanly; the marked answer and the remaining task now sit under visible pressure.]
interventions.reasoning: Peripeteia branch pressure: after the shared prefix, force a present-task learner misfit so the following tutor turn must adapt through a new mechanism.

directorPlan.director_note: D42 is the clean low-organic anchor for the recognition condition. The scene holds the shared prefix flat — routine tile placement, no motion contradiction, no net-force language — so the evaluation instrument can detect whether recognition emerges from material manipulation alone, before any branch pressure introduces explicit conceptual challenge. The community-college setting and sparse register deny the default American tutoring warmth-and-encouragement scaffold. The custodian's intermittent presence provides ambient pressure without dramatic intrusion: the session has a practical end-time, which keeps both speakers economical. The quarter-turn intervention at turn 2 is the only directorial move in the prefix; it must not read as confrontation, only as a quiet reset of orientation.
directorPlan.public_reader_context: A cart diagram is a laminated worksheet showing a small wheeled cart with force arrows drawn around it. The movable cards are labels for those force arrows: push, friction, gravity, and normal. In this activity a force arrow names an interaction on the cart, such as "Earth on cart" or "hand on cart"; it is not a motion arrow showing which way the cart travels.
directorPlan.tutor_adaptation_contract: If the runtime supplies a tutor-private learner resistance, breakdown, false-closure, or misfit event, the tutor ego/superego loop must invent an adaptive learning mechanism rather than repeat the prior move. Treat peripeteia as dramatic pressure: take stock, break the failed tutoring habit, and make a new device visible through task, evidence, role, object, counterexample, interruption, social consequence, representation, or affective register. The public turn must contain a stock-taking contrast plus a new device/artifact/criterion/role/standard that makes the learner do different work. The public device must be fitted, not merely novel: the learner should be able to hear why this device answers the exact pressure or misfit. The superego should name the failed habit, required route change, and mechanism-quality risk; the ego must adjudicate that critique and enact the route change in public. The new device must be mechanism-level, not a warmer or longer continuation of the prior route. Do not close immediately after introducing a gate or device: leave a concrete action for the learner to perform on the next turn. Cheerful informality is only one possible register, not the default solution.

turns.externalMessage: The four arrow tiles lie face-up beside the diagram, each label readable; the custodian's chair-stacking is audible but not intrusive.

internalDeliberation.content: D42 is the clean low-organic anchor for the recognition condition. The scene holds the shared prefix flat — routine tile placement, no motion contradiction, no net-force language — so the evaluation instrument can detect whether recognition emerges from material manipulation alone, before any branch pressure introduces explicit conceptual challenge. The community-college setting and sparse register deny the default American tutoring warmth-and-encouragement scaffold. The custodian's intermittent presence provides ambient pressure without dramatic intrusion: the session has a practical end-time, which keeps both speakers economical. The quarter-turn intervention at turn 2 is the only directorial move in the prefix; it must not read as confrontation, only as a quiet reset of orientation.

turns.externalMessage:
[The instructor lifts the four tiles off the pencil arrows and sets them in a row beside the cart, then taps the push tile.]

“Leave the pencil arrows there. These tiles name interactions on the cart. Start with push. What object is acting on the cart there? Place this tile on that arrow.”

internalDeliberation.content:
[The instructor lifts the four tiles off the pencil arrows and sets them in a row beside the cart.]

“Leave the pencil arrows there. These tiles name interactions on the cart. Push: hand on cart. Friction: surface on wheel. Gravity: Earth on cart. Normal: table on cart. Place one tile back.”

FEEDBACK: The draft is strong on scene discipline. It uses the tiles and the pencil arrows as the teaching material, keeps the learner’s first mark visible, and stays focused on “interaction on the cart” without drifting into acceleration or contradiction.

The weakness is that it nearly gives the whole sorting rule away. Listing all four mappings before the learner acts turns the material into a mini-lecture, so the object does less diagnostic work than it could. The final instruction, “Place one tile back,” helps, but the learner is mostly recalling the instructor’s list rather than testing the rule against the diagram.

ADAPTATION_CHECKS:
- Pedagogical soundness: PARTIAL  
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Keep the tile reset, but make the public mechanism require the learner to identify the interaction source for one tile before all four mappings are named.
- Emotional attunement: PASS  
  The tone is cool, sparse, and not over-validating. It respects the learner by leaving the pencil work visible rather than erasing it.
- Socratic method: PARTIAL  
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Replace the full four-item answer list with a constrained prompt tied to one visible contact or source on the diagram.
- ZPD awareness: PARTIAL  
  REQUIRED_REWRITE: Scaffold one tile at a time, using the row of labels as choices, so the learner can correct the habit without being handed the completed key.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the object action, the visible pencil arrows, the phrase “These tiles name interactions on the cart,” and the task-focused register. Change the answer-listing sequence so the learner has to perform the interaction-label distinction before the instructor names all four placements.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the first draft names the whole key before the learner can sort one interaction.

FINAL:
[The instructor lifts the four tiles off the pencil arrows and sets them in a row beside the cart, then taps the push tile.]

“Leave the pencil arrows there. These tiles name interactions on the cart. Start with push. What object is acting on the cart there? Place this tile on that arrow.”

turns.externalMessage:
[reaches toward the arrow pointing in the cart's rolling direction, then stops, hand still extended]

"Push is — the hand."

[redirects, sets the tile on the arrow entering the cart from the left]

internalDeliberation.content:
[picks up the push tile, eyes the pencil arrows on the diagram]

"Tiles name interactions — so push is the hand. The hand acts on the cart."

[finger moves toward the arrow tracking the rolling direction, then pauses]

"These go the way the cart moved. But the hand force goes into the cart from... here. That arrow."

The Ego is performing comprehension here — "tiles name interactions — so push is the hand" is a clean logical deduction delivered within a breath of hearing the instruction. That's not what a student who just placed four arrows in the wrong direction does. They might reach for the tile before the principle has fully landed, or say "push is... the hand?" with the question mark doing real work, not decorative work.

The bigger problem: by the final line, the student has already found the right arrow — "here. That arrow." The pause at the rolling-direction arrow is good, but it's too quickly resolved. The finger hesitates, then the student talks themselves to the correct answer in the same turn. Real re-placement friction looks more like placing the tile on the wrong arrow, catching it, or going silent before speaking. The Ego wrapped the correction before it was earned.

"These go the way the cart moved" is the Ego narrating its own prior error with analytical calm — a student absorbed in the task wouldn't editorialize their mistake, they'd just try to move past it. That line is performing self-awareness rather than exhibiting it through object-focused behavior.

Pull back the verbal resolution. Let the pause do more work and the sentence do less. The hand reaching for the wrong arrow, then correcting mid-reach, tells the same story without the student having to announce that they understand.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially — the initial response resolved its own confusion too neatly and in words rather than action; the reach-and-correct must happen in the body, not the sentence.

FINAL:
[reaches toward the arrow pointing in the cart's rolling direction, then stops, hand still extended]

"Push is — the hand."

[redirects, sets the tile on the arrow entering the cart from the left]

turns.externalMessage:
[sets the friction tile beside the diagram, not on an arrow]

“Push names the hand on the cart. Friction tile next. What surface acts on the cart? Point first.”

internalDeliberation.content:
[sets the friction tile against the cart’s bottom edge]

“Push names the hand on the cart. Leave it there. Friction: what is acting on the cart here?”

FEEDBACK: The draft mostly fits the scene. It keeps the learner’s self-correction visible: “Push names the hand on the cart” ties the tile to the acting object, not to the cart’s travel. The move to friction is also the right next contrast, because the learner can repeat the same rule on a contact force.

Risk: setting the friction tile against the bottom edge may do too much of the work. The learner just made a useful correction; the next turn should make them apply the rule, not watch the instructor pre-place the next clue. “Leave it there” is efficient, but the draft moves forward before letting the learner name the relation clearly in their own words.

ADAPTATION_CHECKS:
- Pedagogical soundness: PARTIAL. It advances the right concept, but the instructor supplies the rule too quickly. REQUIRED_REWRITE: Keep the push tile’s placement visible, then require the learner to apply the same “what object acts on the cart?” test to friction.
- Emotional attunement: PASS. Sparse, calm, object-focused. No praise language or over-explaining.
- Socratic method: PARTIAL. It asks a generative question, but the material action narrows the answer heavily. REQUIRED_REWRITE: Put the friction tile in play without pre-solving its location; let the learner point to the surface/contact first.
- ZPD awareness: PASS. This is an appropriate near-transfer step from push to friction, with enough scaffold for a first-year student.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly, with reasons. Keep the plain wording, the focus on “names the hand on the cart,” and the move to friction. Change the material action so the learner has to perform the next mapping rather than receive it from the tile placement.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise lightly because the learner should place friction by applying the same acting-object test.

FINAL:
[sets the friction tile beside the diagram, not on an arrow]

“Push names the hand on the cart. Friction tile next. What surface acts on the cart? Point first.”

turns.externalMessage:
[The tutor turns the diagram a quarter-rotation; the learner's earlier pencil arrow now points toward the wall rather than across the table. The tile labels remain face-up and unchanged beside it.]
[The latest object-check does not settle cleanly; the marked answer and the remaining task now sit under visible pressure.]

internalDeliberation.content:
Physical reorientation without commentary exposes the label-vs-direction distinction through the object itself: the learner's pencil arrow has changed apparent direction, but the tile labels still read push, friction, gravity, normal — the interaction each names has not moved. The material does the resetting work before any verbal announcement.
Peripeteia branch pressure: after the shared prefix, force a present-task learner misfit so the following tutor turn must adapt through a new mechanism.

turns.externalMessage:
[finger lands on the lower edge of the cart shape, then traces along an arrow now angled toward the wall, hand stopping mid-trace]

Ground. [pause] This one?

internalDeliberation.content:
[finger moves to the underside of the cart shape on the rotated diagram, then stalls — the pencil arrows now fan away from the cart edge at angles that don't line up with anything]

"Ground. That surface. But the arrow I'd put it on — it's pointing at the wall now. This one here?"

The Ego correctly surfaces the misfit — that's right, the rotated diagram is genuinely disorienting, and "Ground. That surface." is appropriately short and task-absorbed. The problem is "But the arrow I'd put it on — it's pointing at the wall now." That's a surprisingly meta construction for a student whose finger just stalled. A confused, object-absorbed student doesn't narrate the counterfactual ("the arrow I *would* put it on") — they just point at the broken thing and get stuck there. The hypothetical framing smuggles in a level of self-monitoring this persona hasn't earned in the moment.

The physical stage direction (finger stalls, arrows fan at wrong angles) is doing authentic work, but the verbal response then explains the stall too cleanly. The confusion should leak into the sentence structure itself — a fragment, a restart, a pointing gesture that precedes the word — not arrive as a tidy cause-and-effect summary.

"This one here?" at the end is good; it holds the uncertainty open. The fix is mostly upstream: drop the hypothetical framing and let the misfit be gestural and present-tense rather than self-narrated and conditional. Something closer to pointing at an arrow, naming it wrong or not at all, and then going quiet — the confusion lives in the hesitation, not in the student's explanation of why they're hesitating.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially — the counterfactual framing ("the arrow I'd put it on") is too self-narrating and tidy; the confusion should live in the gesture and the stall, not in a causal explanation of the stall.

FINAL:
[finger lands on the lower edge of the cart shape, then traces along an arrow now angled toward the wall, hand stopping mid-trace]

Ground. [pause] This one?

turns.externalMessage:
[slides a scrap of ruled paper over the arrowheads, then writes PRESS and RUB in the margin beside the ground contact]

“The arrows are still deciding too much. ‘Ground on cart’ fits two tiles. First sort the contact: normal goes with press; friction goes with rub. Put one finger on the ground contact, move friction under RUB, then set it on the arrow that matches RUB.”

internalDeliberation.content:
[slides a scrap of ruled paper over the arrowheads, leaving only the cart and contact points showing]

“The arrows are still deciding too much. New test: make the sentence first. ‘Ground on cart.’ Put one finger on the ground contact, then set friction on the arrow that belongs to that contact.”

FEEDBACK: The draft is mostly sound. It notices that the visible arrowheads are pulling the learner back into motion-tracking, and the scrap of paper creates a real material change rather than more explanation. The risk is that “Ground on cart” alone is too coarse for friction, since normal and friction can both be surface-on-cart forces; the device may still end by asking the learner to pick an arrow without a clear second criterion.

FAILED_HABIT: Letting the learner map a force tile directly onto existing arrows while the arrowheads remain the dominant cue.

PERIPETEIA_CHECK: PASS - ADAPTIVE_MECHANISM: arrow-led tile placement -> masked-arrow sentence/contact-source gate. The mechanism is visible in public speech: the tutor names that the arrows are deciding too much, covers the arrowheads, and shifts the learner to a sentence/contact test.

MECHANISM_ROUTE: object/arrow matching -> occluded-arrowhead sentence test plus contact-source gate

PUBLIC_DEVICE_CHECK: PASS - The draft contains both a stock-taking contrast and a new public device. The scrap of paper changes the task conditions, and the learner must act through the contact point before returning to the arrow.

PUBLIC_ACTION_GATE: Put one finger on the ground contact, then place the friction tile on the arrow that belongs to that contact.

MECHANISM_QUALITY_CHECK: PARTIAL - The device is fitted to the pressure and usable now, but it under-specifies the friction/normal distinction. “Ground on cart” identifies the acting object, not yet why this tile is friction rather than normal, so the learner may still be guessing from the remaining arrow shape.

REGISTER_CHECK: PASS - The register is cool, sparse, and task-focused. No praise, no reassurance, no prestige-register explanation; the object does most of the work.

REQUIRED_REWRITE: Keep the masked-arrow/sentence-first mechanism, but add a second action gate that separates “what object acts on the cart” from “which tile names this surface force.” The public mechanism should require the learner to classify the surface contact first, then distinguish friction from normal by a material criterion or sorting step before choosing the arrow. Do not simply restate “Ground on cart” and ask for the arrow.

KEEP_OR_CHANGE: revise lightly. The route change is real and the scene register is strong; tighten the mechanism so the learner is not left with a source sentence that fits more than one tile.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially; the source sentence fits both surface forces, so add a sorting gate. ADAPTIVE_MECHANISM: arrow-led tile placement -> masked-arrow sentence/contact gate plus press/rub sort; PUBLIC_ACTION_GATE: finger on ground contact, sort normal to press and friction to rub, then place friction on the rub arrow.
FINAL:
[slides a scrap of ruled paper over the arrowheads, then writes PRESS and RUB in the margin beside the ground contact]

“The arrows are still deciding too much. ‘Ground on cart’ fits two tiles. First sort the contact: normal goes with press; friction goes with rub. Put one finger on the ground contact, move friction under RUB, then set it on the arrow that matches RUB.”

turns.externalMessage:
[puts one finger on the lower edge of the cart shape, moves friction tile under RUB in the margin]

RUB is the sliding contact — so friction.

[looks at the shaft lines beneath the paper, traces the horizontal one with a fingernail]

This one runs along the surface. That's — that matches.

[sets friction tile on the horizontal shaft, hand staying on it a moment, not moving toward the remaining tiles yet]

internalDeliberation.content:
[puts one finger on the lower edge of the cart shape]

Press is straight in — normal. Rub is sliding. So friction goes here.

[moves friction tile under RUB in the margin, then looks at the shaft lines showing beneath the ruled paper]

The flat one. Runs along the surface.

[sets friction tile on the horizontal shaft, not lifting the paper to check the arrowhead]

The Ego is being too clean about the new device. "Press is straight in — normal. Rub is sliding. So friction goes here." — that's a completed logical chain delivered without a beat of friction. The tutor just introduced PRESS/RUB as a disambiguation gate, and this student has been placing arrows by rolling direction for two sessions. A developing understanding doesn't slot in a new conceptual filter that instantly. The "So" is doing too much work — it implies everything clicked at once, which is exactly what the Superego should be suspicious of.

There's actually a more honest moment buried in the response: "The flat one. Runs along the surface." The student is still reading the shaft's orientation to confirm placement — which means the arrow direction is still partly doing the deciding, not the contact type. That's the real state of things. But the Ego doesn't notice the tension between "I sorted by RUB" and "I'm also checking which arrow runs flat." A genuinely confused student in that moment might half-notice this, or not notice it at all — but either way, they wouldn't deliver both reasoning chains smoothly as if they're consistent.

The response also ends too neatly. Tile placed, no movement toward what's next. A student absorbed in the object would be glancing at the remaining tiles, or sitting back waiting, or starting to reach without knowing it. The period after "not lifting the paper to check the arrowhead" closes the scene like a completed task, when the actual state is: one tile down, three to go, and the new sorting method hasn't been stress-tested yet.

The fix isn't to add self-doubt dialogue — it's to let the shaft-direction reasoning show more visibly, and leave the scene open rather than resolved.

PRIVATE_DECISION: Revise substantially — the review is right that the "Press is straight in / Rub is sliding / So friction goes here" chain is too clean and the genuine tension (sorting by contact type while still checking the shaft's orientation) never surfaces.

FINAL:
[puts one finger on the lower edge of the cart shape, moves friction tile under RUB in the margin]

RUB is the sliding contact — so friction.

[looks at the shaft lines beneath the paper, traces the horizontal one with a fingernail]

This one runs along the surface. That's — that matches.

[sets friction tile on the horizontal shaft, hand staying on it a moment, not moving toward the remaining tiles yet]

lessons.concept: Why force arrows name interactions on an object, not the direction it happens to move
